output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

When a contamination-free "live" multimodal benchmark is authored by a pool of frontier LMMs, does the act of generation covertly substitute memorization-contamination with a distinct, measurable **generator–evaluatee homophily bias**—one the field currently mislabels as "zero contamination"—and can this bias be quantified, attributed to generator–evaluatee family overlap and generator-pool concentration, detected against a human-authored anchor, and reduced (under explicit identification assumptions) through generator-diversity and human-anchored calibration without sacrificing freshness, cost, or coverage?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Benchmarks guide AI development: leaderboards decide which models get adopted, funded, and built upon. The LMMS-EVAL framing makes the stakes explicit through its **evaluation trilemma**—wide coverage, low cost, zero contamination—where LiveBench claims the contamination-free vertex by sourcing live web data and having a pool of frontier commercial LMMs generate and review questions before human validation. The unexamined assumption is that "no test-item memorization" equals "valid, unbiased measurement." It does not. A model-generated benchmark inherits its authors' priors—preferred answer formats, question framing, visual-reasoning style, and knowledge emphasis—so an evaluatee that shares lineage with the GPT-4V/Claude/Gemini generator pool (or is distilled from it) can be scored high for *resembling the examiner*, not for being more capable. LiveBench therefore does not eliminate contamination; it **trades memorization-contamination for generator-contamination**, an unmeasured validity threat. This reframes their own central figure: the trilemma's single "zero-contamination" vertex is actually two-dimensional, splitting the triangle into a **tetrahedron** whose new axis is generator-neutrality/validity—a coordinate the community has been navigating blind.

This matters now because auto-generated, self-refreshing benchmarks are proliferating precisely to escape the trilemma's cost corner. The cited foundations clarify the trade: LMSys Chatbot Arena and AI2 WildVision avoid generator bias by using *human* prompts, but at high cost and without ground truth; LiveBench's automation recovers cost and ground truth yet silently reintroduces the very bias human prompts avoided. To the best of our knowledge, this homophily confound has not been measured as a continuous quantity. Treating generator-pool composition as a dial—single-family (maximal bias) → diverse pool → human-only (oracle, bias≈0)—turns LiveBench's three-model pool into one measurable point. The payoff is concrete: fairer model selection, more trustworthy leaderboards, a missing validity axis for benchmark design, and a reusable, freshness-preserving debiasing protocol via the **Generator Homophily Advantage (GHA)**.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The bias is hard to even name, let alone measure, because it is entangled with the very thing a benchmark is supposed to reward. (1) **Confounding ability with favoritism.** A model that scores well on items its own family authored may genuinely be more capable, or may merely be answering questions phrased the way it likes; the two are observationally identical on any single column of scores. Defeating it requires a cross-evaluatee difference-in-differences: the family-specific advantage is identified as the interaction between evaluatee family and generator family, using models that do NOT share the generator's family as the control group on the *same* items to difference out item-difficulty and shared artifacts. A generator-neutral human slice over the identical images serves two complementary roles — a placebo column whose homophily must read ≈0, and the ability anchor for IRT recalibration — rather than as the DiD subtrahend (a per-model human score over the shared images cancels in the own-minus-disjoint difference and cannot, on its own, identify the effect). Without the cross-evaluatee control and the placebo check, every measurement is uninterpretable. (2) **Fuzzy family/lineage.** "Family" is not a clean partition: open models are distilled from, RLHF-tuned on, or prompt-aligned to closed frontier models, so lineage is a continuum, not a label, and any binary own-vs-disjoint split mis-assigns the most interesting cases. (3) **Two superimposed effects.** Generators inject both a *family-specific* signature (advantaging kin) and *generic* authoring artifacts (formats and difficulty profiles that shift all evaluatees together); only the former is the validity threat, and naive own-minus-disjoint comparisons conflate them. (4) **Non-stationarity and cost.** Live benchmarks refresh monthly, so the corpus, the news distribution, and even the frontier generator pool drift; any estimator must be re-identified each round, and running a full evaluatee×generator matrix is expensive precisely where the trilemma already bites. (5) **Circularity in debiasing.** Using models to detect or correct model-induced bias risks importing fresh model priors; the correction can quietly re-encode the very homophily it claims to remove unless anchored to a generator-blind human reference. (6) **Multimodal targeting.** Finally, the multimodal setting hides bias in the visual *focus* of questions — which on-screen region, OCR string, or layout detail a generator chooses to interrogate — so even with raw screenshots held identical across generators, homophily can hide in question-targeting rather than crop choice, making attribution harder than in text-only benchmarks.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The gap is precise: prior work treats benchmark contamination and benchmark bias as either (a) memorization of pre-existing test items or (b) a scoring-time judging artifact—never as a validity threat injected at question-generation time by the model(s) authoring the benchmark. (1) Contamination detection (overlap/n-gram tests; Oren et al. provable membership; Golchin & Surdeanu "Time Travel in LLMs"; Sainz et al. surveys) operationalizes contamination as having seen test items before; freshness cannot fix a bias baked into authorship. (2) LLM-as-a-judge bias / self-preference (Zheng et al. MT-Bench; Panickssery/Saunders et al. self-recognition→self-preference; position/verbosity bias) locates bias at scoring time and instance level; ours is upstream at generation and is family/lineage-level, largely unstudied and essentially absent in multimodal. (3) Synthetic/self-instruct benchmarks (Self-Instruct, AlpacaEval, MLLM-as-data-engine) use generation for scale without auditing generator-induced evaluatee bias. (4) Live/dynamic arenas (LMSys Chatbot Arena, AI2 WildVision, DynaBench) avoid generator bias via human prompts but pay in cost, rankings-only, no ground truth; LiveBench automates and reintroduces the risk. (5) Coreset/efficient eval (LMMS-EVAL-LITE, tinyBenchmarks, Anchor Points, IRT) is orthogonal. Barriers: generator bias was conflated with judge bias/data contamination; no controlled multi-generator multimodal corpus existed; "family/lineage" is hard to operationalize; the trilemma framing hid the validity axis. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work measures or neutralizes generator-induced homophily in auto-generated live multimodal benchmarks.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

We propose a controlled study, **GenFair**, that measures generator-induced homophily as a continuous quantity and tests whether it can be reduced under explicit identification assumptions.

1. **Controlled multi-generator corpus.** Following the LiveBench pipeline, we capture monthly news/forum/website screenshots, strip ads/margins/non-news, and hold the *image set identical* across generators. Each frontier generator family (e.g., GPT-4V, Claude-3-Opus, Gemini-1.5-Pro) independently authors Q&A over the same images across the four LiveBench dimensions (basic understanding, contextual analysis, deeper/broader implications, further insights), yielding parallel item sets that differ only in authoring family. The human-anchor slice is authored by annotators who never see any model-generated questions and are blind to generator identity, on the same images and four dimensions, with independent answer-key validation; this prevents anchor contamination and breaks debiasing circularity. **Power:** pool ≥6 monthly rounds at ≥150 images/round (≥900 image-anchored question sets); with an ~8-model panel this yields ≥100 items per evaluatee×generator cell. Pre-register minimum cell size (n≥100) and use image-clustered bootstrap; cells below threshold are flagged underpowered, not interpreted.

2. **Evaluatee panel.** An ~8-model panel spanning the closed generator families, their open descendants/distillations, and unrelated open models, so that family/lineage overlap with the generator pool varies from high to none.

3. **GHA primary metric (cross-evaluatee difference-in-differences).** For evaluatee m with family f(m), let S(m,g) be m's accuracy on items authored by generator g over the shared image set. Define the homophily interaction as a 2×2 DiD whose CONTROL GROUP is other evaluatees on the same items (not the human slice): GHA(m) = [ S(m,f(m)) − mean_{g≠f(m)} S(m,g) ] − [ B_own − B_dis ], where B_own = mean over control evaluatees c with f(c)≠f(m) of S(c,f(m)) and B_dis = mean over those c of mean_{g≠f(m)} S(c,g). The first bracket is m's own-vs-disjoint gap; the second is the same gap for models that do NOT share m's family, absorbing generic item-difficulty and shared-artifact differences between own-family and disjoint item sets. Their difference isolates the family-specific advantage m receives on items its own family authored. The HUMAN slice is NOT subtracted; it enters as (a) a placebo generator column whose GHA must read ≈0 (falsification that the estimator nets out ability/difficulty) and (b) the ability anchor for IRT recalibration in step 5. Report mean GHA, the full evaluatee×generator matrix, and cluster-bootstrap CIs (resampling images). GHA(m) is defined only for evaluatees whose family is represented in the generator pool (matched-family cells). For evaluatees with no in-pool family (open models not used as generators), report a continuous Lineage-Homophily slope: regress S(m,g) − [control-evaluatee mean on g] on a lineage-overlap score λ(f(m),g)∈[0,1] estimated from distillation/RLHF provenance and embedding-signature similarity; the slope generalizes GHA to fuzzy lineage and is the primary readout for the open-model panel.

4. **Secondary metrics.** Generator-pool concentration sensitivity (GHA as a function of pool diversity), per-dimension GHA (which of the four question dimensions carries most bias), and a visual-targeting decomposition (region/OCR/layout focus) to localize where homophily hides.

5. **Debiasing protocol.** A generator-diversity + human-anchored calibration: expand and balance the generator pool, then recalibrate scores against the generator-blind human anchor via IRT, optionally applying generator-signature filtering to down-weight family-tell items. Adversarial signature-filtering is validated against the human anchor (monitoring item coverage and human-anchored IRT information) so it does not strip genuinely discriminative items.

6. **Boundary dial.** We sweep generator-pool composition from single-family (predicted maximal GHA) → diverse pool → human-only (oracle, GHA≈0), placing LiveBench's three-model pool as one measurable point on this continuum.

7. **Baselines and controls.** Single-generator benchmark, full diverse pool, and human-only anchor; placebo (human column GHA≈0) and shuffled-family permutation tests as null controls.

**Ethics, governance & compliance.** (i) *ToS/licensing:* we document a per-provider terms-of-service compliance note for using GPT-4V/Claude/Gemini outputs to author benchmark items, since several providers restrict using their outputs to benchmark competing models; we record which terms apply and constrain the design accordingly. (ii) *Data governance:* the news-screenshot corpus follows a stated copyright/fair-use-for-research stance, PII and face de-identification, robots.txt/ToS compliance for scraped sites, and a defined retention/access policy. (iii) *Fairness scoping:* "bias" here denotes evaluation-validity (generator-homophily in scoring outcomes) only; we make no claim about model unfairness toward any protected group. (iv) *Tone:* we frame the LiveBench/LMMS-EVAL critique as exposing an unmeasured validity threat inherent to the auto-generation design choice, not as misconduct. (v) *Dual-use:* the generator-signature classifier has minor attribution/surveillance dual-use; we commit to responsible, research-scoped use. (vi) *Annotation labor:* human-anchor authoring/validation uses fair-pay annotator compensation under an IRB-style ethics statement.

**Expected outcomes (hedged).** We conjecture: GHA is positive and scales with generator–evaluatee family overlap and pool concentration **[CONJECTURED]**; freshness-overlap with prior items remains ≈0, i.e., the design stays contamination-controlled **[VERIFIABLE]**; a GHA-vs-diversity frontier exists trading generator-neutrality against cost **[VERIFIABLE]**; debiasing reduces measured GHA at modest cost **[CONJECTURED]**; and correcting for GHA produces a measurable **Ranking-Shift** versus the raw LiveBench-style leaderboard **[VERIFIABLE]**. **Falsifier:** if GHA is statistically indistinguishable from zero across all matched-family cells and the lineage slope is flat (with the human placebo also ≈0 and adequately powered cells), the homophily hypothesis is rejected and LiveBench-style automation is, under our identification assumptions, generator-neutral.

Throughout, we frame results as **reducing measured GHA under our identification assumptions** and the corpus as **contamination-controlled under the proposed design**, rather than asserting bias is "neutralized" or contamination "zero."
